---
layout: post
title: "Consequences of Field Axioms"
date: 2026-03-27
category: maths
lang: en
lang_pair: "/blog/consequences-of-field-axioms-hindi/"
permalink: /blog/consequences-of-field-axioms-english/
description: "Every familiar arithmetic rule — uniqueness of 0 and 1, a·0=0, (−a)(−b)=ab, no zero divisors, cancellation laws, subtraction and division — proved rigorously from the nine field axioms alone."
---

<style>
  .math-post{font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.9;color:#2d2d2d;max-width:860px;margin:0 auto}
  .post-hero{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72 0%,#2a5298 50%,#7c3aed 100%);border-radius:16px;padding:38px 35px;color:white;margin-bottom:35px;position:relative;overflow:hidden}
  .post-hero::before{content:"⇒";position:absolute;right:20px;top:-10px;font-size:8rem;color:rgba(255,255,255,0.06);font-family:serif}
  .post-hero h1{font-size:1.85rem;font-weight:800;color:white;margin:0 0 10px;line-height:1.3}
  .post-hero p{font-size:1rem;opacity:.9;margin:0 0 14px}
  .lang-switch{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:8px;background:rgba(255,255,255,0.15);border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.35);border-radius:50px;padding:7px 18px;font-size:.85rem;color:white;text-decoration:none;transition:background .2s}
  .lang-switch:hover{background:rgba(255,255,255,0.28);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .stats-strip{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);border-radius:14px;padding:22px 28px;display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;justify-content:space-around;gap:16px;margin-bottom:35px;text-align:center;color:white}
  .stat-item{display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:4px;min-width:110px}
  .stat-val{font-size:1.6rem;font-weight:800}
  .stat-lbl{font-size:.78rem;opacity:.82;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.04em}
  .section-title{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:12px;margin:38px 0 18px;border-bottom:2px solid;border-image:linear-gradient(90deg,#1e3c72,transparent) 1;padding-bottom:6px}
  .sec-icon{width:38px;height:38px;border-radius:10px;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;font-size:1.15rem;flex-shrink:0}
  .section-title h2{font-size:1.35rem;font-weight:800;margin:0;background:linear-gradient(90deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);-webkit-background-clip:text;-webkit-text-fill-color:transparent}
  .info-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:22px 26px;margin:18px 0;box-shadow:0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,.08)}
  .info-card.purple{border-left:5px solid #7c3aed;background:#f5f3ff}
  .info-card.gold{border-left:5px solid #d97706;background:#fffbeb}
  .info-card.green{border-left:5px solid #059669;background:#ecfdf5}
  .info-card.hindi{border-left:5px solid #d97706;background:#fefce8;font-family:'Hind','Noto Sans Devanagari',sans-serif}
  .info-card-header{font-size:.75rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.08em;margin-bottom:10px}
  .info-card-header.purple{color:#7c3aed}
  .info-card-header.gold{color:#d97706}
  .info-card-header.green{color:#059669}
  .axiom-recall{background:#1e3c72;color:white;border-radius:10px;padding:14px 20px;margin:16px 0;font-size:.88rem;line-height:1.9}
  .axiom-recall span{display:inline-block;background:rgba(255,255,255,0.15);border-radius:4px;padding:1px 6px;margin:2px 3px;font-weight:700;font-size:.82rem}
  .cons-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:12px;margin:18px 0}
  .cons-card{background:white;border-radius:10px;padding:14px 16px;border-left:5px solid;box-shadow:0 1px 6px rgba(0,0,0,.07)}
  .cons-num{font-size:.7rem;font-weight:800;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.07em;margin-bottom:4px}
  .cons-result{font-size:.95rem;font-weight:600;color:#0f172a}
  .cons-note{font-size:.8rem;color:#64748b;margin-top:3px}
  .proof-chain{background:#f8fafc;border-left:4px solid #94a3b8;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:16px 20px;margin:12px 0;font-size:.9rem;line-height:1.85}
  .proof-chain p{margin:4px 0}
  .proof-chain .step-label{font-weight:700;color:#1e3c72;display:inline-block;min-width:58px;font-size:.82rem}
  .proof-chain .axiom-tag{background:#e0e7ff;color:#3730a3;border-radius:4px;padding:1px 6px;font-size:.75rem;font-weight:700;font-family:'Courier New',monospace}
  .example-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:24px 26px;margin:22px 0;border-top:5px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,.08);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s}
  .example-card:hover{transform:translateY(-2px);box-shadow:0 6px 22px rgba(0,0,0,.12)}
  .example-card.ex1{border-top-color:#059669}
  .example-card.ex2{border-top-color:#2a5298}
  .example-card.ex3{border-top-color:#7c3aed}
  .example-card.ex4{border-top-color:#dc2626}
  .example-header{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px}
  .example-num{width:36px;height:36px;border-radius:50%;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;color:white;font-weight:800;font-size:1rem;flex-shrink:0}
  .ex1 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#059669,#34d399)}
  .ex2 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2a5298,#60a5fa)}
  .ex3 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa)}
  .ex4 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#dc2626,#f87171)}
  .example-label{font-size:.72rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.08em;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:20px}
  .ex1 .example-label{background:#d1fae5;color:#059669}
  .ex2 .example-label{background:#dbeafe;color:#2a5298}
  .ex3 .example-label{background:#ede9fe;color:#7c3aed}
  .ex4 .example-label{background:#fee2e2;color:#dc2626}
  .example-title{font-weight:700;font-size:1rem;color:#0f172a}
  .solution-block{background:#ecfdf5;border-left:4px solid #059669;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:16px 20px;margin-top:14px}
  .solution-block p{margin:6px 0}
  .warning-box{background:#fef2f2;border-left:5px solid #dc2626;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:20px 24px;margin:18px 0}
  .warning-box h4{color:#dc2626;margin:0 0 12px;font-size:1rem}
  .warning-item{margin:12px 0;padding-left:14px;border-left:2px solid #fca5a5}
  .warning-label{font-weight:700;color:#dc2626;font-size:.88rem}
  .revision-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr);gap:18px;margin:22px 0}
  .rev-card{border-radius:12px;padding:20px 18px;color:white}
  .rev-card.a{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#2a5298)}
  .rev-card.b{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa)}
  .rev-card.c{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#d97706,#f59e0b)}
  .rev-card h4{font-size:.88rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em;margin:0 0 12px;opacity:.9}
  .rev-card ul{margin:0;padding-left:16px;font-size:.9rem;line-height:1.75}
  .app-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fit,minmax(185px,1fr));gap:16px;margin:22px 0}
  .app-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:20px 18px;border-top:4px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 10px rgba(0,0,0,.07);transition:transform .2s}
  .app-card:hover{transform:translateY(-3px)}
  .app-card h4{font-size:.92rem;font-weight:700;margin:8px 0 6px;color:#0f172a}
  .app-card p{font-size:.85rem;color:#475569;margin:0;line-height:1.55}
  .app-icon{font-size:1.6rem}
  .summary-table{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;margin:18px 0;font-size:.88rem}
  .summary-table th{background:#1e3c72;color:white;padding:10px 12px;text-align:left;font-size:.8rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em}
  .summary-table td{padding:9px 12px;border-bottom:1px solid #e2e8f0;vertical-align:top}
  .summary-table tr:nth-child(even) td{background:#f8fafc}
  .highlight-result{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);border-radius:14px;padding:26px 30px;text-align:center;color:white;margin:28px 0}
  .highlight-result p{margin:0;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.75}
  .social-footer{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#0f172a,#1e3c72);color:white;border-radius:14px;padding:28px 30px;margin:40px 0 20px;text-align:center}
  .social-footer a{color:#93c5fd}
  .social-footer p{margin:8px 0}
  .pdf-section{text-align:center;margin:44px 0 28px}
  .pdf-btn{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1B2A4A,#2A5298);color:white;padding:15px 36px;border-radius:6px;font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1rem;font-weight:700;box-shadow:0 4px 18px rgba(27,42,74,.40);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn:hover{transform:translateY(-3px);box-shadow:0 8px 26px rgba(27,42,74,.50);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn .pi{font-size:1.5rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt{display:flex;flex-direction:column;text-align:left;line-height:1.35}
  .pdf-btn .pt1{font-size:1rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt2{font-size:.72rem;opacity:.82;font-weight:400}
  @media(max-width:700px){.cons-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr}.revision-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr}}
</style>

<div class="math-post">

<!-- §1 HERO + STATS -->
<div class="post-hero">
  <h1>Consequences of Field Axioms</h1>
  <p>You know that $(-2)(-3)=6$ and that you cannot divide by zero. But <em>why?</em> Every familiar rule of arithmetic — subtraction, division, sign changes, cancellation — is not an assumption: it is a <strong>theorem</strong>, derived purely from the nine field axioms. This post gives full axiomatic proofs of every consequence, with every step labelled by the axiom it invokes.</p>
  <a class="lang-switch" href="/blog/consequences-of-field-axioms-hindi/">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में पढ़ें</a>
</div>

<div class="stats-strip">
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">16</span><span class="stat-lbl">Consequences</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">9</span><span class="stat-lbl">Axioms used</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">0</span><span class="stat-lbl">Extra assumptions</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Solved Examples</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">CSIR/GATE/JAM</span><span class="stat-lbl">Exam Relevance</span></div>
</div>

<!-- §2 AXIOMS RECALLED -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ede9fe;">&#x1F4CB;</div>
  <h2>The Nine Axioms — Recalled</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Axioms of $(F, +, \cdot)$ — Reference Card</div>
  <p>Everything in this post is derived from exactly these nine statements. No other properties of $\mathbb{Q}$ or $\mathbb{R}$ are used.</p>
</div>

<div class="axiom-recall">
  <span>(A1)</span> $x+y=y+x$ &nbsp;&nbsp;
  <span>(A2)</span> $(x+y)+z=x+(y+z)$ &nbsp;&nbsp;
  <span>(A3)</span> $\exists\,0\colon 0+x=x$ &nbsp;&nbsp;
  <span>(A4)</span> $\exists\,{-x}\colon x+(-x)=0$<br>
  <span>(M1)</span> $xy=yx$ &nbsp;&nbsp;
  <span>(M2)</span> $(xy)z=x(yz)$ &nbsp;&nbsp;
  <span>(M3)</span> $\exists\,1\neq0\colon 1\cdot x=x$ &nbsp;&nbsp;
  <span>(M4)</span> $x\neq0\Rightarrow\exists\,x^{-1}\colon xx^{-1}=1$<br>
  <span>(D)</span> $x(y+z)=xy+xz$
</div>

> 📖 **Reference:** Rudin, W., *Principles of Mathematical Analysis*, 3rd ed., Ch. 1, Propositions 1.14–1.16. Also: Apostol, T.M., *Mathematical Analysis*, 2nd ed., Ch. 1, §1.3–1.4.

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F517; Prerequisites</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/algebraic-structures-and-field-axioms-english/"><strong>Algebraic Structures and Field Axioms</strong></a> — the nine axioms (A1–A4, M1–M4, D) are assumed known from this post; every consequence here is derived from them.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — every proof here is a direct axiomatic argument or uses proof by contradiction.</p>
</div>

<!-- §3 INTUITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#d1fae5;">&#x1F4A1;</div>
  <h2>Intuition — Theorems, Not Assumptions</h2>
</div>

<p>When we work in $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{Q}$, we freely use rules like $(-a)(-b)=ab$ or "you cannot cancel if $a=0$." But these are not self-evident truths — they are <strong>theorems that follow logically from the nine axioms</strong>. Proving them from scratch has two purposes: it shows that any field (including exotic finite fields $\mathbb{F}_p$ used in cryptography) must obey exactly the same rules, and it gives practice in the axiomatic method central to all of real analysis.</p>

<div class="info-card green">
  <div class="info-card-header green">&#x1F50D; The Master Proof Strategy</div>
  <p><strong>Uniqueness results</strong> — show two elements satisfy the same equation, then use cancellation to prove they are equal.</p>
  <p><strong>Zero-product results</strong> — use $0+0=0$ (A3), distribute (D), then add inverses (A4) to cancel.</p>
  <p><strong>Sign rules</strong> — show $(-a)b + ab = 0$ using (D); invoke uniqueness of additive inverse.</p>
  <p><strong>No zero divisors</strong> — assume $ab=0$, $a\neq0$; multiply by $a^{-1}$ (M4); conclude $b=0$.</p>
  <p><strong>Rule of thumb:</strong> Every step in a field proof must cite an axiom label or a consequence already proved — no "it is obvious" shortcuts.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card hindi">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में संक्षेप</div>
  <p>अंकगणित के सभी परिचित नियम — $a\cdot0=0$, $(-a)(-b)=ab$, शून्य-भाजक नहीं, लघुकरण नियम — <strong>प्रमेय (Theorems)</strong> हैं, अभिगृहीत नहीं। इन्हें केवल नौ Field अभिगृहीतों (A1–A4, M1–M4, D) से सिद्ध किया जाता है। प्रत्येक चरण में उपयोग किए गए अभिगृहीत का लेबल देना अनिवार्य है। घटाव $a-b:=a+(-b)$ और भाग $a/b:=a\cdot b^{-1}$ परिभाषित संक्रियाएँ हैं, मूल अभिगृहीत नहीं।</p>
</div>

<!-- §4 THE 16 CONSEQUENCES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ede9fe;">&#x1D53D;</div>
  <h2>All Sixteen Consequences</h2>
</div>

<p>Let $F$ be a field and $a, b, c, d \in F$. Every result below follows from the nine axioms alone.</p>

<div class="cons-grid">
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#2a5298;">Result 1 — Uniqueness of 0</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$a+b=a \;\Rightarrow\; b=0$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Add $-a$ both sides; use A2, A4, A3.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#2a5298;">Result 2 — Uniqueness of $-a$</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$a+b=0 \;\Rightarrow\; b=-a$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Follows from Result 1 applied to $(-a)+(a+b)$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#7c3aed;">Result 3 — Uniqueness of 1</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$ab=a$, $a\neq0 \;\Rightarrow\; b=1$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Multiply by $a^{-1}$; use M2, M4, M3.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#7c3aed;">Result 4 — Uniqueness of $a^{-1}$</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$ab=1$, $a\neq0 \;\Rightarrow\; b=a^{-1}$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Same strategy: multiply by $a^{-1}$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#059669;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#059669;">Result 5 — Double Negative</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$-(-a) = a$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">$(-a)+a=0$; by Result 2, $-(-a)=a$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#059669;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#059669;">Result 6 — Zero Product</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$a \cdot 0 = 0$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">D on $0+0=0$; then additive cancel (Result 1).</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#d97706;">Result 7 — Sign of Product</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$(-a)b = -(ab)$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Show $ab+(-a)b=0$ via D, A4, Result 6; use Result 2.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#d97706;">Result 8 — Neg × Neg</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$(-a)(-b) = ab$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Apply Result 7 with $b \leftarrow -b$; use Result 5.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#dc2626;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#dc2626;">Result 9 — No Zero Divisors</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$ab=0 \;\Rightarrow\; a=0$ or $b=0$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">$a\neq0$: multiply by $a^{-1}$; M2, M4, M3, Result 6.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#dc2626;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#dc2626;">Result 10 — Multiply by $-1$</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$(-1)\cdot a = -a$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">$a+(-1)a=(1+(-1))a=0\cdot a=0$; invoke Result 2.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#6366f1;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#6366f1;">Result 11 — Additive Cancellation</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$a+b=a+c \;\Rightarrow\; b=c$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Add $-a$; use A2, A4, A3. No condition on $a$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#6366f1;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#6366f1;">Result 12 — Mult. Cancellation</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$ab=ac$, $a\neq0 \;\Rightarrow\; b=c$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Multiply by $a^{-1}$; M2, M4, M3. Requires $a\neq0$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#0891b2;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#0891b2;">Result 13 — Subtraction</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$a - b \;:=\; a + (-b)$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Definition; $a-a=0$; $-(a-b)=b-a$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#0891b2;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#0891b2;">Result 14 — Division</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$\dfrac{a}{b} \;:=\; a \cdot b^{-1}$, $b\neq0$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Definition; $(a/b)\cdot b=a$; $a/1=a$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#059669;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#059669;">Result 15 — Fraction Addition</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$\dfrac{a}{b}+\dfrac{c}{d}=\dfrac{ad+bc}{bd}$, $b,d\neq0$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">Derived from D, M1, M2, M4, and $(bd)^{-1}=b^{-1}d^{-1}$.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="cons-card" style="border-left-color:#059669;">
    <div class="cons-num" style="color:#059669;">Result 16 — Fraction Multiplication</div>
    <div class="cons-result">$\dfrac{a}{b}\cdot\dfrac{c}{d}=\dfrac{ac}{bd}$, $b,d\neq0$</div>
    <div class="cons-note">$(ab^{-1})(cd^{-1})=ac\cdot b^{-1}d^{-1}=ac(bd)^{-1}$.</div>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §4 SELECTED FULL PROOFS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#dbeafe;">&#x1F4DD;</div>
  <h2>Selected Full Proofs with Axiom Citations</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F9EE; Proof of Result 6: $a \cdot 0 = 0$</div>
  <div class="proof-chain">
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 1.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">A3</span> $\;\Rightarrow\;$ $0 + 0 = 0$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 2.</span> Multiply both sides by $a$: $a(0+0)=a\cdot0$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 3.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">D</span> $\;\Rightarrow\;$ $a\cdot0+a\cdot0=a\cdot0$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 4.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">Result 1</span> (uniqueness of $0$): the only $x$ with $a\cdot0+x=a\cdot0$ is $x=0$. Here $x=a\cdot0$, so $a\cdot0=0$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F9EE; Proof of Result 7: $(-a)b = -(ab)$</div>
  <div class="proof-chain">
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 1.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">D</span>: $ab + (-a)b = (a + (-a))b$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 2.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">A4</span>: $a + (-a) = 0$, so $(a+(-a))b = 0 \cdot b$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 3.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">M1</span> + <span class="axiom-tag">Result 6</span>: $0\cdot b = b\cdot 0 = 0$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 4.</span> So $ab + (-a)b = 0$. By <span class="axiom-tag">Result 2</span> (uniqueness of $-ab$): $(-a)b = -(ab)$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F9EE; Proof of Result 9: No Zero Divisors</div>
  <div class="proof-chain">
    <p><span class="step-label">Given:</span> $ab = 0$ and $a \neq 0$. We prove $b = 0$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 1.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">M4</span>: since $a\neq0$, $a^{-1}$ exists.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 2.</span> $a^{-1}(ab) = a^{-1}\cdot0$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 3.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">M2</span>: LHS $= (a^{-1}a)b$. <span class="axiom-tag">M4</span>: $a^{-1}a=1$. <span class="axiom-tag">M3</span>: $1\cdot b=b$. So LHS $= b$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 4.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">Result 6</span>: RHS $= a^{-1}\cdot0=0$. Hence $b=0$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F9EE; Proof of Result 12: Multiplicative Cancellation</div>
  <div class="proof-chain">
    <p><span class="step-label">Given:</span> $ab = ac$ and $a \neq 0$. We prove $b = c$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 1.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">M4</span>: $a^{-1}$ exists. Multiply: $a^{-1}(ab) = a^{-1}(ac)$.</p>
    <p><span class="step-label">Step 2.</span> <span class="axiom-tag">M2</span>, <span class="axiom-tag">M4</span>, <span class="axiom-tag">M3</span>: both sides simplify — LHS $=b$, RHS $=c$. Hence $b=c$. $\blacksquare$</p>
    <p><em>Note:</em> If $a=0$: $0\cdot b=0=0\cdot c$ for all $b,c$ — cancellation fails. The condition $a\neq0$ is essential.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §5 SOLVED EXAMPLES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x270F;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Solved Examples</h2>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex1">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">1</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Very Easy &nbsp;|&nbsp; Direct Application</div>
      <div class="example-title">Show $0 \cdot 0 = 0$ in any field, citing the relevant consequence</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Apply Result 6</strong> ($a\cdot0=0$ for all $a\in F$) with $a=0$:</p>
    <p>$$0 \cdot 0 = 0. \quad \blacksquare$$</p>
    <p>No additional work is needed; this is an immediate substitution into an already-proved consequence.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex2">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">2</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Easy–Medium &nbsp;|&nbsp; Uniqueness</div>
      <div class="example-title">Suppose $e \in F$ satisfies $e + x = x$ for all $x \in F$. Prove $e = 0$.</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p>Setting $x = 0$: $e + 0 = 0$.</p>
    <p>By <strong>A3</strong>: $e + 0 = e$, so $e = 0$.</p>
    <p><em>Alternatively</em>, using <strong>Result 1</strong> directly: $e + 0 = 0$ means $e + 0 = 0$; since also $0 + 0 = 0$ (A3), we have $e + 0 = 0 + 0$, so by additive cancellation (Result 11): $e = 0$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex3">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">3</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Medium–Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; Full Axiomatic Proof</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove $(-a)(-b) = ab$ in any field, citing every axiom</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Step 1.</strong> Prove $(-a)b = -(ab)$ (Result 7):</p>
    <p>$ab + (-a)b \stackrel{\text{D}}{=} (a+(-a))b \stackrel{\text{A4}}{=} 0\cdot b \stackrel{\text{M1, R6}}{=} 0$. So $(-a)b = -(ab)$ by <strong>Result 2</strong>.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 2.</strong> Apply Step 1 with $b \leftarrow (-b)$: $(-a)(-b) = -(a(-b))$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 3.</strong> Apply Step 1 again (with original $a, -b$): $a(-b) = -(ab)$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 4.</strong> $(-a)(-b) = -(-(ab)) \stackrel{\text{R5}}{=} ab$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex4">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">4</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; CSIR NET / GATE / IIT JAM Level</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove Results 15 and 16 — fraction addition and multiplication from axioms</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <p>Let $b, d \neq 0$. Prove: (i) $\dfrac{a}{b}+\dfrac{c}{d}=\dfrac{ad+bc}{bd}$; (ii) $\dfrac{a}{b}\cdot\dfrac{c}{d}=\dfrac{ac}{bd}$.</p>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Proof of (i).</strong></p>
    <p>$\dfrac{a}{b}+\dfrac{c}{d} = ab^{-1}+cd^{-1}$ (by definition, Result 14).</p>
    <p>Insert $1=dd^{-1}=bb^{-1}$ via <strong>M4</strong> and <strong>M3</strong>:</p>
    <p>$= ab^{-1}(dd^{-1}) + cd^{-1}(bb^{-1})$</p>
    <p>$\stackrel{\text{M1,M2}}{=} (ad)(b^{-1}d^{-1}) + (cb)(d^{-1}b^{-1})$</p>
    <p>$\stackrel{\text{M1}}{=} (ad)(bd)^{-1} + (bc)(bd)^{-1}$ &nbsp; (since $(bd)^{-1}=b^{-1}d^{-1}$ by M1)</p>
    <p>$\stackrel{\text{D}}{=} (ad+bc)(bd)^{-1} = \dfrac{ad+bc}{bd}$. $\blacksquare$</p>
    <p><strong>Proof of (ii).</strong></p>
    <p>$\dfrac{a}{b}\cdot\dfrac{c}{d} = (ab^{-1})(cd^{-1}) \stackrel{\text{M1,M2}}{=} ac\cdot b^{-1}d^{-1} = ac(bd)^{-1} = \dfrac{ac}{bd}$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §6 QUICK REVISION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#dbeafe;">&#x26A1;</div>
  <h2>Quick Revision Cards</h2>
</div>

<div class="revision-grid">
  <div class="rev-card a">
    <h4>&#x1F4CA; A — Consequences 1–10</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>$a+b=a \Rightarrow b=0$ (uniq. $0$)</li>
      <li>$a+b=0 \Rightarrow b=-a$ (uniq. $-a$)</li>
      <li>$-(-a)=a$</li>
      <li>$a\cdot0=0$; &nbsp; $0\cdot0=0$</li>
      <li>$(-a)b=-(ab)$; &nbsp; $(-a)(-b)=ab$</li>
      <li>$ab=0\Rightarrow a=0$ or $b=0$</li>
      <li>$(-1)a=-a$</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card b">
    <h4>&#x2699;&#xFE0F; B — Consequences 11–16 &amp; Traps</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Additive cancel: $a+b=a+c\Rightarrow b=c$ (always)</li>
      <li>Mult. cancel: $ab=ac$, $a\neq0\Rightarrow b=c$</li>
      <li>$a-b:=a+(-b)$; &nbsp; $a/b:=ab^{-1}$</li>
      <li>$a/b+c/d=(ad+bc)/(bd)$</li>
      <li>$a/b\cdot c/d=ac/(bd)$</li>
      <li>$(ab)^{-1}=b^{-1}a^{-1}=a^{-1}b^{-1}$</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card c">
    <h4>&#x1F3AF; C — Exam Tips</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>&#x1F535; <strong>CSIR NET:</strong> "Prove $a\cdot0=0$" — cite D on $0+0=0$; cancel. Full marks need axiom labels.</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E2; <strong>GATE:</strong> Mult. cancel requires $a\neq0$; add. cancel has no such condition — MCQ trap.</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E0; <strong>IIT JAM:</strong> Fraction rules are derived, not assumed — know the full proof from axioms.</li>
      <li>&#x1F534; <strong>B.Sc. Raj.:</strong> List results 1–10 as a theorem; cite axiom at every step.</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §7 COMMON MISTAKES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fee2e2;">&#x26A0;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Common Mistakes</h2>
</div>

<div class="warning-box">
  <h4>&#x274C; Errors to Avoid</h4>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 1: Circular proofs</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Using "$a-b=0\Rightarrow a=b$" to prove cancellation (this is what we are trying to prove). &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> Start from $a+b=a+c$; add $-a$ using A4; simplify with A2, A4, A3 to reach $b=c$.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 2: Multiplicative cancellation without $a \neq 0$</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> From $ab=ac$ concluding $b=c$ without checking $a\neq0$. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> If $a=0$, then $0\cdot b=0=0\cdot c$ for any $b,c$, so cancellation fails completely. The condition is not cosmetic.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 3: Treating subtraction and division as axioms</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "We use the subtraction axiom $a-b=a+(-b)$." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> There is no subtraction axiom. $a-b$ is a definition — it abbreviates $a+(-b)$. Similarly $a/b$ abbreviates $a\cdot b^{-1}$.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 4: Writing $a/0$ or assuming $0^{-1}$ exists</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Dividing by zero in an argument. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $b^{-1}$ is defined only for $b\neq0$ (axiom M4). In any field, $0\cdot x=0\neq1$ for all $x$, so $0$ has no multiplicative inverse and $a/0$ is undefined.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §8 APPLICATIONS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ecfdf5;">&#x1F310;</div>
  <h2>Real-World Applications</h2>
</div>

<div class="app-grid">
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4BB;</div>
    <h4>Computer Algebra Systems</h4>
    <p>Every CAS (Mathematica, SageMath, SymPy) uses $(-a)(-b)\to ab$ and $a\cdot0\to0$ as rewrite rules applied millions of times per second in symbolic computation.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4D0;</div>
    <h4>Linear Algebra over Fields</h4>
    <p>Row reduction, determinants, and eigenvalues work over any field. No-zero-divisors ensures $AB=0$ with $A$ invertible forces $B=0$ — the foundation of rank-nullity theory.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F9EE;</div>
    <h4>Rational Function Arithmetic</h4>
    <p>Results 15 and 16 (fraction addition and multiplication) are the exact rules for adding and multiplying rational functions. Partial fraction decomposition depends on uniqueness of field inverses.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F512;</div>
    <h4>Coding Theory (AES/BCH)</h4>
    <p>Operations in $\mathbb{F}_{2^n}$ (used in AES, BCH codes) implement exactly these derived rules. No-zero-divisors guarantees that encoded messages can always be uniquely recovered.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §9 SUMMARY TABLE -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#e0e7ff;">&#x1F4CB;</div>
  <h2>Summary Table &amp; Key Principle</h2>
</div>

<table class="summary-table">
  <thead>
    <tr><th>#</th><th>Result</th><th>Key proof step</th><th>Rudin ref.</th></tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td>1–2</td><td>Uniqueness of $0$ and $-a$</td><td>Add $-a$; A2, A4, A3</td><td>P1.14(i–ii)</td></tr>
    <tr><td>3–4</td><td>Uniqueness of $1$ and $a^{-1}$</td><td>Multiply by $a^{-1}$; M2, M4, M3</td><td>P1.14(iii–iv)</td></tr>
    <tr><td>5</td><td>$-(-a)=a$</td><td>$(-a)+a=0$; invoke Result 2</td><td>P1.14(v)</td></tr>
    <tr><td>6</td><td>$a\cdot0=0$</td><td>D on $0+0=0$; Result 1</td><td>P1.14(vi)</td></tr>
    <tr><td>7–8</td><td>$(-a)b=-(ab)$; $(-a)(-b)=ab$</td><td>D, A4, Result 6; then Result 5</td><td>P1.14(vii)</td></tr>
    <tr><td>9</td><td>No zero divisors</td><td>Multiply by $a^{-1}$; M4</td><td>P1.14(viii)</td></tr>
    <tr><td>10</td><td>$(-1)a=-a$</td><td>$a+(-1)a=0$; Result 2</td><td>P1.15</td></tr>
    <tr><td>11–12</td><td>Additive and mult. cancellation</td><td>Add/mult. by inverse; A4/M4</td><td>P1.14</td></tr>
    <tr><td>13–14</td><td>Subtraction and division defined</td><td>Definitions: $a-b:=a+(-b)$; $a/b:=ab^{-1}$</td><td>P1.15</td></tr>
    <tr><td>15–16</td><td>Fraction arithmetic</td><td>D, M1, M2, $(bd)^{-1}=b^{-1}d^{-1}$</td><td>P1.15–16</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div class="highlight-result">
  <p><strong>The Key Principle:</strong></p>
  <p>Every familiar arithmetic rule is a <strong>theorem</strong>, not an axiom.</p>
  <p>The nine field axioms (A1–A4, M1–M4, D) logically force all sixteen consequences — nothing is assumed beyond them.</p>
</div>

<!-- §10 CROSS-REFERENCES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x1F517;</div>
  <h2>Cross-References &amp; Related Posts</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F4DA; Prerequisites &amp; Next Steps</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Direct prerequisite:</strong> <a href="/blog/algebraic-structures-and-field-axioms-english/"><strong>Algebraic Structures and Field Axioms</strong></a> — the nine axioms from which all sixteen consequences here are derived.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Proof toolkit:</strong> <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — every proof here is a direct axiomatic argument or uses proof by contradiction.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2192; Next Topic:</strong> <em>The Real Number Field and Ordered Fields</em> — adding the order axioms (O1–O3) to the field axioms, and the Completeness Axiom that makes $\mathbb{R}$ unique among ordered fields.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x1F4D6; Further Reading:</strong> Rudin, Ch. 1, Prop. 1.14–1.16; Apostol, Ch. 1, §§1.3–1.5; Bartle &amp; Sherbert, Ch. 2, §2.1.</p>
</div>
